explanation_md: `p` holds the address of `x`. Assigning `*p = 20` dereferences `p` and writes 20 directly into `x`. When `std::cout << x` runs, `x` is already 20, not 5.

`p` starts pointing to `a` (value 3). Then `p = &b` reassigns the pointer — it now points to `b`. Dereferencing `*p` gives the value stored at `b`, which is 7. Unlike references, pointers can be reseated to point to different objects.

prompt_md: In the expression `int* p = &x;`, what does the `&` operator do?

options.c: Dereferences the pointer to get `x`'s value
options.d: Creates a copy of `x` and stores it in `p`

explanation_md: When `&` appears in an expression (not in a type declaration), it is the address-of operator. `&x` produces the memory address where `x` is stored, which is then assigned to the pointer `p`. It does not dereference anything or make a copy.

options.a: `int*` must be initialized with `new int` before use
options.b: `p` is uninitialized — it holds a garbage address, so `*p = 99` writes to an unknown memory location
options.c: You cannot print a dereferenced pointer with `std::cout`
options.d: The type should be `int& p` instead of `int* p`

explanation_md: An uninitialized pointer holds whatever random bits are in memory at that location. Dereferencing it (`*p = 99`) attempts to write to whatever address those bits happen to encode — this is undefined behavior and typically causes a crash. Always initialize a pointer to `nullptr` or to the address of a valid variable before dereferencing.

prompt_md: What is the key difference between an lvalue reference (`int& r = x`) and a pointer (`int* p = &x`)?

options.a: A reference stores the address; a pointer stores the value
options.b: A reference can be rebound to a different variable; a pointer cannot
options.c: A pointer can be reassigned to point to a different object; a reference is permanently bound to its initial object
options.d: Dereferencing a reference requires `*r`; dereferencing a pointer requires just `p`

explanation_md: Once a reference is initialized to a variable, it is permanently bound to that variable for its entire lifetime — you cannot reseat it. A pointer is just a variable holding an address; you can assign a new address to it at any time (`p = &y`). Dereferencing a pointer uses `*p`; using a reference is transparent and requires no special syntax.
